topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt like sandpaper against my fingertips as I unfolded the document. Every line, every carefully chosen anecdote about my childhood, my triumphs, my supposed struggles, was a calculated performance. A performance I hadn't auditioned for. A cold, sharp pressure built behind my ribs, tightening until I could barely draw a breath. I reread the section detailing my 'defining moments,' and a tremor ran through my hands, making the paper flutter dangerously. How dare they? These words, belonging to me, were being wielded as weapons against me. I crumpled the paper slowly, deliberately, the sound of the tearing sound echoing too loudly in the silence of my apartment. I needed to burn it all, to erase the ink, but the gesture felt hollow, a pathetic attempt to control the sheer, corrosive feeling churning in my gut.

[story 2]

My eyes scanned the printed narrative, and the color drained from my face until I felt translucent. It wasn't just that the facts were wrong; it was the tone—the condescending pity woven into every sentence. A deep, visceral resentment began to coil in my stomach, a heavy, suffocating weight. I stood up abruptly, knocking over a stack of books. The noise was a small release, a physical manifestation of the internal storm raging within me. "This is a lie," I whispered, the sound raspy and brittle. I paced the small room, my movements jerky and agitated, my jaw clenched so tightly my teeth ached. Every shadow seemed to deepen, every corner of the room felt accusatory, as if the very air was judging the inadequacy of my existence as presented in this fabricated history.

[story 3]

I sat at the mahogany desk, the document spread before me like a battlefield map. My breath hitched in shallow, rapid bursts. The realization struck me not as a shock, but as a profound, sickening betrayal. A slow, burning heat started to spread from my chest outward, making my skin prickle uncomfortably. I looked at the names, the dates, the carefully curated narrative of my life, and a wave of pure, unadulterated fury washed over me. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the desk, the tendons standing out like cords under the strain. I didn't move to destroy it immediately; instead, I simply stared, letting the sheer, unvarnished indignation simmer, a slow-burning ember threatening to consume everything around it.
